title: List, Set, Map and Queue

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for List, Set, Map and Queue; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Generics and Collections Framework learning paths; atomic topic: List, Set, Map and Queue
sources.claim: Java collection interfaces encode different contracts: List preserves positional sequence, Set uniqueness, Map key-value association, and Queue ordered removal policy; implementations add performance and ordering traits.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java collection interfaces encode different contracts: List preserves positional sequence, Set uniqueness, Map key-value association, and Queue ordered removal policy; implementations add performance and ordering traits.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A word counter uses Map<String,Integer>, while a unique insertion-ordered report uses LinkedHashSet rather than a List plus repeated scans. Your goal is to select and use an appropriate collection for a stated problem and justify its trade-offs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Interfaces; Arrays and multidimensional arrays; Generic types and methods; pf data selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java collection interfaces encode different contracts: List preserves positional sequence, Set uniqueness, Map key-value association, and Queue ordered removal policy; implementations add performance and ordering traits. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Hash iteration order is not a sorting promise, equality/hashing govern uniqueness and keys, null policies vary, mutability may be restricted, and big-O guarantees depend on implementation and workload. The learner must reject this documented misconception: ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap, and PriorityQueue differ only in method names and can be swapped without changing semantics.

includes: Use collection interfaces and choose implementations by ordering, uniqueness, lookup and mutation needs.
Select and use an appropriate collection for a stated problem and justify its trade-offs.
excludes: Hash iteration order is not a sorting promise, equality/hashing govern uniqueness and keys, null policies vary, mutability may be restricted, and big-O guarantees depend on implementation and workload.

outcomes: Select and use an appropriate collection for a stated problem and justify its trade-offs.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Choose the interface contract first—order, duplicates, lookup, or removal policy—then select an implementation whose guarantees and cost fit the operations.

examples.title: List, Set, Map and Queue: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A priority queue retrieves the least element by its comparator but does not expose the entire collection in sorted iteration order.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A priority queue retrieves the least element by its comparator but does not expose the entire collection in sorted iteration order.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Hash iteration order is not a sorting promise, equality/hashing govern uniqueness and keys, null policies vary, mutability may be restricted, and big-O guarantees depend on implementation and workload.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap, and PriorityQueue differ only in method names and can be swapped without changing semantics.
